claims.text: In 1908 President Teddy Roosevelt sought to close the Grand Canyon to timber and mining operations, a process that took Congress another 11 years to complete.

evidence.quote:
in 1908 president Teddy Roosevelt wanted
to declare the Grand Canyon off limits
to all Timber and Mining operations it
would take another 11 years for Congress
to

claims.rationale: The Grand Canyon was declared a national monument by Roosevelt's proclamation in January 1908 and became a national park by act of Congress in February 1919 — an ~11-year gap matches the historical record.

claims.text: Explorer G.E. Kincaid claimed he found a man-made cave entrance in the Grand Canyon whose walls bore Ancient Egyptian hieroglyphics.

evidence.quote:
saw
writing but it wasn't English or Native
American writing it was Ancient Egyptian
Hieroglyphics Kincaid lifted his
flashlight and saw that the tunn

claims.rationale: This claim is the crux of the 1909 Phoenix Gazette story. It has never been independently verified — no artifacts, no cave, no Kincaid records. The video later establishes it is generally considered a hoax.

claims.text: Kincaid estimated the underground complex could have housed as many as 50,000 people and contained granaries, cooking areas, and metal-work rooms.

evidence.quote:
now
realized he was in a huge complex he
estimated that as many as 50 000 people
could have lived here in some rooms he
found granaries with shelves of g

claims.rationale: Source is Kincaid's own newspaper account, which has never been corroborated. Treated as part of the hoax narrative by the video's own later reporting.

claims.text: Kincaid reported that Professor S.A. Jordan of the Smithsonian arrived with a team of about 40 scientists, researchers, and laborers to excavate the cave.

evidence.quote:
ogical
Discovery ever made the Smithsonian
agreed a few weeks later Professor S.A
Jordan arrived with a team of about 40
scientists researchers and laborers to
excav

claims.rationale: This detail is central to the 1909 Gazette story. The Smithsonian in later interviews (see c_0008) says no one named S.A. Jordan or G.E. Kincaid ever worked there.

claims.text: The Hopi, Navajo, Zuni, Apache, and other tribes have lived around the Grand Canyon for several thousand years.

evidence.quote:
lived in the area the Hopi Navajo
Zuni Apache and several other tribes
have lived around the Grand Canyon for
several thousand years they still do
Ho

claims.rationale: Archaeological and ethnographic consensus confirms multi-millennial indigenous habitation of the Colorado Plateau including the Grand Canyon region.

claims.text: Hopi mythology holds that a reptilian race emerged from the Grand Canyon thousands of years ago and changed into humans.

evidence.quote:
the Grand
Canyon the first people of the world
entered the story says that many
thousands of years ago a reptilian race
emerged
yep hey how many times do lizard p

claims.rationale: The host is reporting a claimed element of Hopi emergence mythology; actual Hopi cosmology speaks of emergence through a sipapu from prior worlds, and 'reptilian' framing is closer to a New-Age reading than ethnographic record.

claims.text: The entire Kincaid cave story originates from two 1909 Phoenix Gazette front-page articles that mainstream researchers have since concluded were a hoax.

evidence.quote:
rmation in today's episode
comes from Two Front Page articles from
the Phoenix Gazette published in 1909
and the years since mainstream
researchers have decided that the
Articles were a hoax a fantastic story
designed to sell newspapers in 19th
century clickbait yep the media hasn't
changed much unfortunately

claims.rationale: The two April 1909 Phoenix Gazette articles are the sole primary source and are considered a journalistic hoax by mainstream archaeologists and skeptics. No secondary sourcing ever surfaced.

claims.text: The Smithsonian confirmed over the phone that no record exists of a G.E. Kincaid ever working there and called the Grand Canyon Egyptian-cave story a hoax.

evidence.quote:
nything
like this in the Grand Canyon or
anywhere so GE Kincaid
who recorded anyone by that name ever
working for the Smithsonian the whole
thing is a hoax yeah

claims.rationale: The Smithsonian's public-relations office has consistently denied any employment record for Kincaid or Jordan and characterized the story as a hoax, matching this on-camera phone call.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] the Smithsonian's own records rule out the alleged workers, undermining the cave-discovery narrative.
[debunks] no Professor Jordan or Kincaid is in the Smithsonian employment record.

claims.text: Researcher Jerry Wills and his wife Kathy claim they located the likely Kincaid cave entrance after years of studying the original expedition's base camp.

they say they found it

years their approach was find the
original base camp of the Explorers

claims.rationale: Wills and his wife credibly claim to have found the site, but no cave, artifacts, or hieroglyphics have been produced.

claims.text: Rock formations inside the Grand Canyon carry ancient-Egyptian and Hindu-themed names including Isis Temple, Tower of Set, Horus, the Cheops Pyramid, Manu Temple, and Buddha Temple.

evidence.quote:
o in this forbidden zone rock
formations have unusual names there is
Isis Temple Tower of set Horus the kiops
pyramid and raw there's also the Manu
Temple the Buddha temple the Kri

claims.rationale: These are real USGS-mapped named rock formations in the Grand Canyon, assigned by geologist Clarence Dutton in the 1880s — the names exist regardless of any occult interpretation.

claims.text: Author David Hatcher Childress claims the Smithsonian cover-up of advanced pre-Columbian civilizations began in 1881 when geologist John Wesley Powell appointed Cyrus Thomas to head the Bureau of Ethnology's Eastern Mound division.

evidence.quote:
discoveries
made in the Americas he says the
cover-up began in 1881 with famous
geologist John Wesley Powell Powell
appointed Cyrus Thomas the director of
the Eastern Mo

claims.rationale: Powell did appoint Thomas to lead the Mound Survey in 1881 — Thomas's report argued that Mound Builders were ancestral to Native Americans. Framing this as the start of a 'cover-up' is Childress's interpretation, not standard history.

claims.text: At the Spiro Mounds (excavated in the 1930s) seven- and eight-foot tall skeletons were reportedly found, but the remains have since been lost.

evidence.quote:
urials of
extremely tall people sometimes seven or
eight feet tall when the Spiro Mounds
were excavated in the 1930s seven and
eight foot tall skeletons were found at
a time when t

claims.rationale: Spiro Mounds is a real Oklahoma archaeological site excavated in the 1930s. Reports of giant skeletons there are a staple of fringe literature but do not appear in the published WPA-era excavation reports; bones of normal size are well documented.
